title: On the Ozsváth–Szabó invariant of negative definite plumbed 3-manifolds
overall_summary: The paper studies connected negative definite plumbing trees Γ with lattice L generated by the vertices, diagonal entries e_j, and off-diagonal entries 1 along edges. Its main topological result is a computation of Heegaard Floer homology for almost-rational plumbing graphs via graded roots, but the most transferable material is the combinatorial lattice theory developed en route: anti-nef cycles, canonical characteristic elements, χ-functions, Artin rationality, and Laufer computation sequences. A central theme is that if all but at most one vertex satisfy the rationality-type inequality −e_j ≥ δ_j, then the graph is almost-rational and the rational parts of the graph are controlled by monotone computation sequences. For the original problem, after reversing the sign of the positive definite form, the hypothesis that one vertex has weight less than degree is exactly the paper’s one-bad-vertex condition, so the anti-nef minimization and Laufer-sequence arguments are the most relevant extraction.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 2.1 dual-basis sign observation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a connected plumbing graph whose bilinear form B on the free abelian group L with basis {b_j}_{j∈J} is negative definite, with B(b_j,b_j)=e_j and B(b_i,b_j)=1 when vertices i and j are adjacent and 0 otherwise. Let L' = Hom_Z(L,Z), identify L' with a sublattice of L⊗Q via B, and let {g_j}_{j∈J} be the dual basis, so B(g_j,b_i)=δ_{ij}. Then −g_j is an effective rational cycle for every j∈J; equivalently, every entry of the inverse matrix B^{-1} is nonpositive.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper records this as clear from negative definiteness. In the notation later used in the paper, x=−g_j satisfies B(x,b_i)=−δ_{ij}≤0 for every i, so x lies in the anti-nef cone S_Q={y∈L⊗Q : B(y,b_i)≤0 for all i}. Theorem 6.1(a) proves that every element of this cone is coefficientwise nonnegative. Thus −g_j≥0. Matrix-theoretically this is the standard inverse-sign property for a negative definite tree intersection matrix with nonnegative off-diagonal entries.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a connected negative definite plumbing graph with lattice L and let k be any characteristic element, meaning k∈L' and k(x)+B(x,x) is even for every x∈L. Define χ_k:L→Z by χ_k(x)=−(k(x)+B(x,x))/2. For each n∈Z form a one-dimensional simplicial complex L_{k,≤n} whose vertices are lattice points x∈L with χ_k(x)≤n and whose edges join x to x+b_j whenever both endpoints have χ_k≤n. Let R_k have as vertices the connected components of all L_{k,≤n}, with an edge from a component at level n to a containing component at level n+1. Then R_k, graded by the level n, is a graded root: adjacent levels differ by 1, local branching is upward, each level has finitely many vertices, the grading is bounded below, and for n sufficiently large there is exactly one component.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The construction gives edges only between adjacent levels. The no-downward-branching condition follows because if a component C_u at a lower level lay inside two distinct higher-level components C_v and C_w, then those two higher-level components would intersect in the nonempty connected set C_u, impossible. Negative definiteness makes χ_k proper and bounded below, so each sublevel has finitely many lattice points and the level sets of components are finite. For eventual connectedness, the paper introduces the finite set Y of lattice points x such that χ_k(x)≤χ_k(x±b_j) for every basis element b_j; equivalently, x satisfies finitely many inequalities −χ_k(−b_j)≤B(x,b_j)≤χ_k(b_j). This set is finite by nondegeneracy and negative definiteness. Choose one y_*∈Y and fixed lattice paths from y_* to every other y∈Y, and let n_0 be the maximum χ_k-value along these paths. Any point not in Y has a neighboring point with no larger χ_k, and repeated descent reaches Y; hence every point in L_{k,≤n} for n≥n_0 connects to y_* inside L_{k,≤n}. Thus high sublevels are connected.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a connected negative definite plumbing graph with lattice L and rational extension L_Q=L⊗Q. Define the anti-nef cone S_Q={x∈L_Q : B(x,b_j)≤0 for every vertex j}. For any coset l'+L⊂L_Q, the intersection (l'+L)∩S_Q has a unique coefficientwise minimal element, denoted l'_[k] in the paper when the coset corresponds to a spin-c structure [k].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Existence comes from choosing a sufficiently large effective integral cycle y with B(y,b_j)<0 for all j, so l'+Ny lies in S_Q for N large. For uniqueness and minimality, the key closure property is that the coordinatewise minimum of two anti-nef representatives in the same coset is again anti-nef. If l'+l_1 and l'+l_2 are in S_Q, set l=min(l_1,l_2) coefficientwise and write x_i=l_i−l≥0. The supports of x_1 and x_2 are disjoint. For any vertex b_j, at least one of x_1,x_2 has zero coefficient at b_j; say b_j is not in the support of x_1. Then B(l'+l,b_j)=B(l'+l_1,b_j)−B(x_1,b_j)≤0, because B(l'+l_1,b_j)≤0 and B(x_1,b_j)≥0 when x_1 is effective and does not contain b_j in its support. Therefore l'+l∈S_Q. Taking repeated coordinatewise minima yields a unique minimal element.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix a spin-c class [k] represented as K+2(l'+L), where K is the canonical characteristic element defined by K(b_j)=−e_j−2. Let l'_[k] be the unique minimal element of (l'+L)∩S_Q from Lemma 5.4, and put k_r=K+2l'_[k]. Then: (a) B(l'_[k],b_j)≥b_j^2+1 for every vertex j; (b) k_r(x)≥B(x,x) for every effective integral cycle x≥0; and (c) χ_{k_r}(−x)≥0 for every effective integral cycle x≥0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (a), if B(l'_[k],b_{j0})≤b_{j0}^2 for some j0, then l'_[k]−b_{j0} remains in the same coset and is still anti-nef: the j0-pairing becomes B(l'_[k],b_{j0})−b_{j0}^2≤0, and all other pairings do not increase because off-diagonal intersections are nonnegative. This contradicts minimality of l'_[k]. For (b), first (a) implies k_r(b_j)=2B(l'_[k],b_j)−b_j^2−2≥b_j^2 for each basis vector. If some effective x>0 had k_r(x)<B(x,x), choose a coefficientwise minimal such x. Then for each b_j in the support of x, minimality gives k_r(x−b_j)≥B(x−b_j,x−b_j), while k_r(x)<B(x,x). Subtracting yields B(l'_[k]−x,b_j)≤0 for every b_j in the support. For b_j outside the support, B(l'_[k]−x,b_j)≤0 follows from l'_[k]∈S_Q and x≥0. Hence l'_[k]−x∈(l'_[k]+L)∩S_Q, contradicting minimality. Part (c) is the identity 2χ_{k_r}(−x)=k_r(x)−B(x,x), together with (b).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 6.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a connected negative definite plumbing graph. For a spin-c class [k], let l'_[k] be the minimal anti-nef representative from Lemma 5.4, let k_r=K+2l'_[k], and define S_[k]={x∈L : B(x+l'_[k],b_j)≤0 for every j}. Let L_{k,≤n} be the χ_k-sublevel complex, and let L^s_{k,≤n} be the subcomplex spanned by L_{k,≤n}∩S_[k]. Then: (a) if x∈S_[k], then x≥0 coefficientwise; (b) the inclusion L^s_{k,≤n}→L_{k,≤n} induces a surjection on connected components for every n; (c) for k=K, the connected component C_0 of L_{K,≤0} containing 0 contains no nonzero effective cycle, and every nonzero cycle in C_0 is coefficientwise negative and has χ=0; (d) for k=K, the complex L_{K,≤n} is connected for every n≥1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (a), write x=x_1−x_2 with x_1≥0, x_2≥0, and disjoint supports. If b_j is in the support of x_2, then B(l'_[k]−x_2,b_j)≤B(l'_[k]+x,b_j)≤0; if b_j is not in the support of x_2, then B(l'_[k]−x_2,b_j)≤B(l'_[k],b_j)≤0. Thus l'_[k]−x_2 lies in the same coset and in S_Q, so minimality of l'_[k] forces x_2=0. For (b), if x is not in S_[k], choose j with B(x+l'_[k],b_j)≥1. Then χ_k(x+b_j)≤χ_k(x). Repeating this step gives an increasing sequence of cycles with nonincreasing χ_k. Properness from negative definiteness forces termination, and the endpoint lies in S_[k], so each component of L_{k,≤n} meets L^s_{k,≤n}. For (c), first note that b_j is never in L_{K,≤0}, while −b_j may be if e_j=−1. Then induct along paths in C_0 starting from 0: if x<0 and χ(x)=0, and x±b_j also lies in C_0, the case x+b_j with b_j outside the support would give χ(x+b_j)=1+B(−x,b_j)≥1, impossible in L_{K,≤0}. Thus negativity is preserved, and Proposition 5.7(c) forces χ=0. For (d), by (a) and (b) it is enough to connect every effective x with χ(x)≤n to 0 inside L_{K,≤n}. Two reduction facts are proved. If x>0 and χ(x)>0, some b_j in the support satisfies χ(x−b_j)≤χ(x); otherwise B(b_j,x−b_j)>1 for all support vertices, implying B(K+x,x)≥0 and χ(x)≤0, contradiction. If x>0 and χ(x)≤0, some support vertex satisfies χ(x−b_j)≤1; otherwise, writing c=χ(x)≤0 and N=sum of coefficients of x, the inequalities χ(x−b_j)>1 imply B(K+x,x)+cN>0, equivalently c(N−2)>0, impossible. These steps lower coefficients while staying within the sublevel n whenever n≥1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 6.2 Artin rationality criterion and closure facts
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a connected negative definite plumbing graph Γ with canonical χ(x)=−(K(x)+B(x,x))/2, the graph is called rational if χ(x)≥1 for every nonzero effective integral cycle x>0. This is Artin’s topological characterization of resolution graphs of rational singularities. The paper also records that any plumbing graph with −e_j≥δ_j for every vertex j is rational, where δ_j is the valency of j, and that the class of rational graphs is closed under taking subgraphs and under decreasing Euler decorations e_j.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The rationality criterion is cited from Artin. For the sufficient condition −e_j≥δ_j, the cycle sum_j b_j is anti-nef because B(sum_i b_i,b_j)=e_j+δ_j≤0. It is the Artin fundamental cycle, and a direct computation gives χ of this fundamental cycle equal to 1; by Artin’s criterion this implies rationality. Closure under subgraphs and decreasing Euler numbers is stated in the paper as a standard consequence: deleting vertices or making diagonal entries more negative preserves the inequalities and, more generally, preserves the positivity of χ on nonzero effective cycles.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 6.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a connected negative definite plumbing graph whose plumbed 3-manifold is a rational homology sphere, let K be the canonical characteristic element, and let (R_can,χ_can) be the graded root associated with χ=χ_K. Then the following conditions are equivalent: Γ is rational; χ_can^{-1}(0) has exactly one element; R_can is the standard ray R_0; R_can is a standard ray R_m for some integer m; for every characteristic element k, R_k is a standard ray R_{m_k}; equivalently H(R_can)=T^+_0, equivalently H(R_can)=T^+_m for some m, equivalently H(R_k)=T^+_{m_k} for all k. Moreover, if Γ is rational and k=K+2l' is any characteristic element, then min_x χ_k(x)=χ(l'_[k])−χ(l')=χ_k(l'_[k]−l')≤0, where l'_[k] is the minimal anti-nef representative of the coset l'+L; in particular, for the distinguished representative k_r=K+2l'_[k], one has min χ_{k_r}=0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If Γ is rational, then χ(x)≥1 for every effective x>0. By Theorem 6.1(a,b), every component of a χ-sublevel has an effective anti-nef representative, and the only effective point with χ≤0 is 0; Theorem 6.1(c,d) then force the canonical graded root to be exactly R_0. The implications from a standard ray to the corresponding H-module statements are formal from the definition of H(R). Conversely, if R_can is any standard ray, then χ_can^{-1}(0) has only the component containing 0. Thus L_{K,≤0} equals the component C_0 from Theorem 6.1(c), which contains no nonzero effective cycle, so χ(x)>0 for every x>0 and the graph is rational by Artin’s criterion. For the statement about arbitrary k, it is enough, using the shift relation between representatives in a fixed spin-c class, to take k=K+2l'_[k]. For any effective x>0, if no support vertex b_j satisfied χ_k(x−b_j)≤χ_k(x), then B(x+l'_[k],b_j)>1+b_j^2 for every support vertex. Since B(l'_[k],b_j)≤0, this gives B(K+x,b_j)≥0 on the support, hence χ(x)≤0, contradicting rationality. Therefore every effective x can be reduced to 0 through nonincreasing χ_k, and Theorem 6.1(b) handles non-effective points; hence min χ_k=0 for the distinguished representative. The formula for general l' follows from χ_k(x)=χ(l'+x)−χ(l') and the definition of l'_[k].

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 7.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix a connected negative definite plumbing graph Γ, a spin-c class [k], its distinguished representative k_r=K+2l'_[k], and S_[k]={x∈L : B(x+l'_[k],b_j)≤0 for every j}. For every x∈L there exists a unique coefficientwise minimal y=y(x)∈L such that x≤y and y∈S_[k].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Existence is obtained by choosing an effective cycle y_0>0 with B(y_0,b_j)<0 for every j and taking a large multiple above x; adding enough y_0 makes all pairings B(x+Ny_0+l'_[k],b_j) nonpositive. For uniqueness, use the same coordinatewise-minimum argument as Lemma 5.4: if y_1,y_2∈S_[k], then min(y_1,y_2) is again in S_[k]. Hence the set of all anti-nef cycles above x is closed under coordinatewise minimum and has a unique minimal element.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 7.4, generalized Laufer algorithm
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: With Γ, [k], k_r, l'_[k], and S_[k] as in Lemma 7.3, fix x∈L. Define a sequence x_0,x_1,... as follows: x_0=x; if x_l is not in S_[k], choose a vertex j with B(x_l+l'_[k],b_j)>0 and set x_{l+1}=x_l+b_j; stop when x_l∈S_[k]. Then the algorithm terminates at the minimal element y(x) from Lemma 7.3. Moreover χ_{k_r}(x_{l+1})≤χ_{k_r}(x_l) at every step.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let y(x) be the minimal anti-nef cycle above x. The proof shows inductively that x_l≤y(x) until termination. If x_l≤y(x) and the algorithm chooses b_j, then the coefficient of b_j in x_l must be strictly smaller than in y(x); otherwise b_j is not in the support of y(x)−x_l, and B(x_l+l'_[k],b_j)=B(y(x)+l'_[k],b_j)−B(y(x)−x_l,b_j)≤0, contradicting the choice of j. Thus x_l+b_j≤y(x). Since the sequence is monotone increasing and bounded above by y(x), it terminates, and minimality forces the terminal point to equal y(x). The χ-change is χ_{k_r}(x_l+b_j)−χ_{k_r}(x_l)=1−B(x_l+l'_[k],b_j), which is ≤0 because the chosen pairing is a positive integer.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 7.5, Artin fundamental cycle and rational Laufer sequences
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For k=K and starting point x=b_j, the endpoint y(x) of the generalized Laufer algorithm is Artin’s fundamental cycle x_min, the minimal nonzero effective cycle satisfying B(x_min,b_i)≤0 for every i. If Γ is rational, then any computation sequence from a basis element b_j to x_min as in Lemma 7.4 has χ constantly equal to 1; equivalently, at every step x_{l+1}=x_l+b_{j(l)} one has B(x_l,b_{j(l)})=1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The first assertion is the definition of the fundamental cycle in this language: start with any vertex and add vertices with positive intersection until all intersections are nonpositive. For a rational graph, Artin’s criterion gives χ(x)>0 for every effective x>0, and in particular χ(b_j)=1 and χ(x_min)=1. Lemma 7.4 gives χ(x_{l+1})≤χ(x_l) along the sequence. Since all intermediate cycles are nonzero effective and the endpoints have χ=1, all inequalities must be equalities. The identity χ(x_l+b_{j(l)})−χ(x_l)=1−B(x_l,b_{j(l)}) then gives B(x_l,b_{j(l)})=1 at every step.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 7.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix a distinguished vertex b_0 and write J={0}∪J*. For a spin-c class [k] with distinguished representative k_r=K+2l'_[k], and for each integer i≥0, there is a unique cycle x(i)∈L with the following properties: the coefficient m_0(x(i)) of b_0 is i; B(x(i)+l'_[k],b_j)≤0 for every j∈J*; and x(i) is coefficientwise minimal among cycles satisfying these two properties. Moreover x(i)≥0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Existence is established by taking a sufficiently large anti-nef cycle above b_0, multiplying it so that rational denominators in l'_[k] disappear, and then subtracting a suitable multiple of b_0 to force the b_0-coefficient to equal i while preserving the nonpositive pairings against vertices in J*. Uniqueness again follows from coordinatewise-minimum closure, now restricted to cycles with fixed b_0-coefficient. To prove x(i)≥0, write x(i)=x_1−x_2 with x_1,x_2≥0 and disjoint supports. Since the b_0-coefficient is i≥0, the negative part x_2 has no b_0 component. For j∈J*, if b_j is outside the support of x_1, then B(l'_[k]−x_2,b_j)≤B(l'_[k]+x(i),b_j)≤0; if b_j is in the support of x_1, then B(l'_[k]−x_2,b_j)≤B(l'_[k],b_j)≤0. The b_0-pairing is also nonpositive, so l'_[k]−x_2∈S_Q. Minimality of l'_[k] forces x_2=0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 7.7 and 7.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the setting of Lemma 7.6, there is a computation sequence connecting x(i) to x(i+1): start with x_0=x(i), set x_1=x(i)+b_0, and then, while some j∈J* has B(x_l+l'_[k],b_j)>0, set x_{l+1}=x_l+b_j; stop when all pairings against J* are nonpositive. The endpoint is x(i+1). For all steps after the initial addition of b_0, χ_{k_r} is nonincreasing. The initial change is χ_{k_r}(x(i)+b_0)−χ_{k_r}(x(i))=1−B(x(i)+l'_[k],b_0), so χ increases at the initial step exactly when x(i) lies in the full anti-nef set S_[k]. More generally, if x has b_0-coefficient i and x≤x(i), then applying the same algorithm using only vertices in J* terminates at x(i) and χ_{k_r} is nonincreasing throughout.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof repeats Lemma 7.4 with the coefficient of b_0 fixed after the first step. The inductive invariant is that every intermediate cycle stays below the minimal cycle x(i+1), or below x(i) in the generalized version. If the chosen vertex b_j had already reached its coefficient in the target minimal cycle, the nonpositive target pairing and nonnegative off-diagonal intersections would contradict B(x_l+l'_[k],b_j)>0. Hence the addition is allowed and remains below the target. Termination follows from monotonicity and boundedness. The χ formula is again χ(x_l+b_j)−χ(x_l)=1−B(x_l+l'_[k],b_j), and for j∈J* the chosen positive pairing makes this nonpositive.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 8.1 and Examples 8.2, almost-rational graphs
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A connected negative definite plumbing tree Γ is called almost-rational, or AR, if there is a vertex j_0 such that replacing its decoration e_{j0} by some smaller or equal integer e'_{j0}≤e_{j0} produces a rational graph. Rational graphs are AR. Weakly elliptic graphs are AR. Every star-shaped negative definite graph is AR. More generally, the paper notes that the graphs considered by Ozsváth and Szabó with at most one vertex at which the inequality −e_j≥δ_j fails are AR: decrease the decoration at that exceptional vertex until all vertices satisfy −e_j≥δ_j, which is a sufficient condition for rationality. The class of AR graphs is closed under taking subgraphs and under decreasing Euler decorations.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a graph with at most one bad vertex, choose that vertex as j_0. Decreasing e_{j0} makes −e_{j0} as large as needed, so eventually every vertex satisfies −e_j≥δ_j. Section 6.2 states that this condition implies rationality, hence the original graph is AR. Star-shaped graphs are handled similarly after blowing down irrelevant −1 vertices: take the central vertex and make its decoration sufficiently negative. Closure under subgraphs and decreasing decorations follows because rational graphs have the same closure properties, and the same distinguished vertex or its induced version can be used.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 9.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume Γ is an almost-rational graph with distinguished vertex b_0, and fix a spin-c class [k] with distinguished representative k_r=K+2l'_[k]. Let x(i) be the minimal cycles from Lemma 7.6. Then for every i≥0: (a) if x∈L has b_0-coefficient i, then χ_{k_r}(x)≥χ_{k_r}(x(i)); (b) if y>x(i) and y has the same b_0-coefficient i, then there exists a vertex b_j in the support of y−x(i), with j∈J*, such that χ_{k_r}(y−b_j)≤χ_{k_r}(y); (c) in any computation sequence connecting x(i) to x(i+1) as in Lemma 7.7, every step after the initial b_0-step has B(x_l+l'_[k],b_{j(l)})=1, equivalently χ_{k_r}(x_{l+1})=χ_{k_r}(x_l). In particular χ_{k_r}(x(i)+b_0)=χ_{k_r}(x(i+1)).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (a), write x=x(i)−y_1+y_2 where y_1,y_2≥0, neither contains b_0, and their supports are disjoint. Expanding χ gives χ_k(x)=χ_k(x(i)−y_1)+χ(y_2)+B(y_1,y_2)−B(x(i)+l'_[k],y_2). The last two terms are nonnegative: B(y_1,y_2)≥0 by disjoint support and nonnegative edge intersections, and −B(x(i)+l'_[k],y_2)≥0 by the defining inequalities for x(i). The support of y_2 lies in the rational graph obtained after modifying the distinguished vertex, or in a rational subgraph, so χ(y_2)≥0. Lemma 7.8 then gives χ_k(x(i)−y_1)≥χ_k(x(i)). For (b), suppose every b_j in the support of y−x(i) gave χ_k(y−b_j)>χ_k(y). This is equivalent to B(y+l'_[k],b_j)≥2+b_j^2. Since B(x(i)+l'_[k],b_j)≤0, one obtains B(y−x(i),b_j)≥2+b_j^2, or B(y−x(i)+K,b_j)≥0, on the support. Summing over the effective cycle y−x(i) gives χ(y−x(i))≤0, contradicting rationality of the supporting subgraph. For (c), let z_l=x_l−x(i) along the computation sequence. For l>0 the chosen vertex lies in J*, and B(z_l,b_{j(l)})=B(x_l+l'_[k],b_{j(l)})−B(x(i)+l'_[k],b_{j(l)})>0. These intersection numbers are unchanged when the distinguished decoration is decreased to make the graph rational. In the resulting rational graph, the sequence can be completed to a Laufer sequence for the Artin fundamental cycle, so Remark 7.5 forces B(z_l,b_{j(l)})=1. The displayed identity then gives B(x_l+l'_[k],b_{j(l)})=1 and χ equality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 9.2 and Theorem 9.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be an almost-rational graph with distinguished vertex b_0, fix [k] and k_r=K+2l'_[k], and let (R_k,χ_k) be the graded root associated with χ_k. If v is a local minimum vertex of R_k and C_v is the corresponding connected component of a χ_k-sublevel complex, then C_v has a unique coefficientwise maximal element x_v, this element lies in S_[k], and if i_v is its b_0-coefficient, then the minimal slice cycle x(i_v) lies in C_v. Consequently, if τ(i)=χ_k(x(i)) for i≥0, then τ is eventually nondecreasing, the graded root R_τ built from the sequence τ is well-defined, and (R_k,χ_k) is isomorphic to (R_τ,χ_τ).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Lemma 9.2, local minimality of v implies that all lattice points in C_v have the same χ-value. If x, x+b_i, and x+b_j lie in C_v with i≠j, then direct computation gives χ_k(x+b_i+b_j)=χ_k(x)−B(b_i,b_j)≤χ_k(x), so local minimality forces x+b_i+b_j also into C_v. This replacement operation rules out two distinct maximal elements and gives uniqueness of x_v. Since χ strictly increases when adding any b_j to x_v, the formula χ_k(x_v+b_j)−χ_k(x_v)=1−B(x_v+l'_[k],b_j) implies B(x_v+l'_[k],b_j)≤0, hence x_v∈S_[k]. The minimality defining x(i_v) gives x(i_v)≤x_v; Lemma 9.1(b) constructs a path from x(i_v) upward to x_v along which χ does not increase, and local minimality forces it to stay in C_v. For Theorem 9.3, local minima of R_k inject into the sequence of slice minimizers x(i), and every strict down-up pattern in τ gives a local minimum. Since R_k has finitely many local minima, τ is eventually nondecreasing. The same argument identifies local minima of R_k and R_τ with the same χ-values. For two local minima with indices i_u<i_v, any path between the corresponding cycles must pass through a cycle with b_0-coefficient i for each intermediate i, and Lemma 9.1(a) shows the maximal χ along such a path is at least max_{i_u≤i≤i_v}χ_k(x(i)); the concatenated computation sequences from Lemma 7.7 and Lemma 9.1(c) realize this bound. Thus the join levels in the two graded roots agree, giving an isomorphism.

proof_techniques.name: Anti-nef cone and coordinatewise-min closure
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly studies the anti-nef cone S_Q={x : B(x,b_j)≤0 for all j}. A fundamental device is that within a fixed coset l'+L, the coordinatewise minimum of two anti-nef representatives is again anti-nef, because off-diagonal intersections are nonnegative and the supports of the two positive differences from the minimum are disjoint. This gives unique minimal anti-nef representatives and unique minimal cycles with prescribed coefficient at a distinguished vertex.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: After sign reversal, the original positive definite tree has the same matrix pattern as −B. Minimal anti-nef representatives can be used to choose canonical cycles or extremal decompositions, potentially forcing a vertex with an indecomposability property.

proof_techniques.name: Laufer computation sequence
proof_techniques.description: Given a cycle not satisfying anti-nef inequalities, the paper adds a vertex b_j with positive pairing B(x+l',b_j)>0. The key identity χ(x+b_j)−χ(x)=1−B(x+l',b_j) shows that χ does not increase. Negative definiteness prevents infinite descent inside a sublevel, and minimality arguments show that the process stops at the unique minimal anti-nef cycle above the starting point.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a systematic way to turn local intersection inequalities into global combinatorial conclusions. In the original problem, if one assumes all vertices decompose, analogous sequences may help build a forbidden effective cycle or a contradiction to positive definiteness.

proof_techniques.name: Rational graph control via Artin’s criterion
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses Artin’s criterion that a negative definite graph is rational exactly when χ(x)≥1 for every nonzero effective cycle x. The easy sufficient condition −e_j≥δ_j at every vertex makes the sum of all vertices anti-nef and gives a rational graph. On rational graphs, Laufer sequences from a vertex to the fundamental cycle have χ identically 1, forcing every added vertex to have intersection exactly 1 with the current cycle.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the original graph has a unique bad vertex, then deleting or isolating that vertex leaves pieces satisfying the rationality inequality after sign reversal. The exact-intersection property on those pieces is a strong combinatorial constraint likely useful for locating an irreducible vertex.

proof_techniques.name: One-bad-vertex isolation through almost-rational graphs
proof_techniques.description: An almost-rational graph is one that becomes rational after decreasing the decoration at a single distinguished vertex. The paper observes that graphs with at most one failure of −e_j≥δ_j are almost-rational, because making the exceptional decoration sufficiently negative forces the sufficient rationality inequalities everywhere. This isolates all nonrational behavior at one vertex and lets the rational complement control computation sequences.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original hypothesis, under the sign convention B=−(original form), is exactly a one-bad-vertex hypothesis. Thus the proof can try to distinguish that vertex and exploit rationality of the rest rather than analyze the whole tree uniformly.

proof_techniques.name: Fixed-coefficient slice minimization
proof_techniques.description: With a distinguished vertex b_0, the paper defines x(i) as the minimal cycle whose b_0-coefficient is i and whose pairings with all other vertices are nonpositive. In almost-rational graphs, x(i) minimizes χ among all cycles with that b_0-coefficient. Any larger cycle in the same slice can be reduced by subtracting a non-distinguished vertex without increasing χ. This converts a multidimensional lattice problem into a one-dimensional sequence indexed by i.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a tree with one exceptional vertex, fixing the coefficient of that vertex may reduce potential decompositions or extremal cycles to a controlled family on rational slices. This can bypass the difficulty created by the single bad vertex.

proof_techniques.name: Sublevel-set and graded-root viewpoint
proof_techniques.description: The paper encodes the topology of χ-sublevel sets L_{k,≤n} into a graded root. Negative definiteness ensures finite sublevels and eventual connectedness; local minima of the root correspond to special lattice cycles. In the almost-rational case, all relevant local minima are represented by the slice minimizers x(i), and the whole graded root is recovered from the sequence χ(x(i)).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Even if Heegaard Floer theory is not used, the idea of studying connected components of quadratic-form sublevel sets may reveal extremal vertices or cycles. An irreducible vertex could be sought among cycles representing local minima or unavoidable transitions in such a filtration.

proof_techniques.name: Dual-basis sign and inverse-matrix positivity after sign reversal
proof_techniques.description: For the negative definite plumbing form, the dual basis satisfies −g_j≥0. This is an inverse-matrix sign theorem for the intersection matrix. It is used implicitly to control rational cycles and anti-nef representatives.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original positive definite form with off-diagonal −1 along edges, the inverse matrix should have nonnegative entries. Such positivity can be useful for choosing extremal vertices or proving that certain coefficient patterns in a putative decomposition cannot occur.

other_useful_info: The paper works in the negative definite plumbing convention: diagonal entries are e_j and edge intersections are +1, and the bad-vertex inequality is the failure of −e_j≥δ_j. The original problem’s lattice is the sign-reversal of this convention. The paper itself does not discuss irreducible lattice elements, but its one-bad-vertex framework, Artin rationality criterion, and Laufer computation sequences are directly aligned with the original hypothesis. Relevant cited sources for the combinatorial singularity-theory tools are Artin [1,2] for rational graphs and Laufer [5,6] for computation sequences and elliptic/rational cycle algorithms.
why_relevant: Develops graded-root and lattice methods for negative-definite plumbed trees, including the bad-vertex framework. The Laufer-sequence style arguments are useful for extracting combinatorial consequences from definiteness.
